coredata.dc:title: A novel magnetic fluorescent molecularly imprinted sensor for highly selective and sensitive detection of 4-nitrophenol in food samples through a dual‐recognition mechanism
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Wenting
Zhou, Yushun
Liu, Shuai
Luo, Mei
Du, Jun
Fan, Jieping
Xiong, Hua
Peng, Hailong
coredata.dc:description: In this study, surface imprinting, magnetic separation, and fluorescent detection were integrated to develop a dual-recognition sensor (MF-MIPs), which was used for highly selective and sensitive detection of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) in food samples. Silane-functionalized carbon dots (Si-CDs) participated in the imprinting process and were uniformly distributed into the MIPs layers. MF-MIPs sensor exhibited a high fluorescence response and selectivity based on the dual-recognition mechanism of imprinting recognition and fluorescence identification. The relative fluorescence intensity of MF-MIPs sensor presented a good linear relationship in the range of 0.08–10 μmol·L−1 with a low limit of detection (23.45 nmol·L1) for 4NP. MF-MIPs sensor showed high anti-interference, as well as excellent stability and reusability. The 4-NP recovery from spiked food samples ranged from 93.20 to 102.15%, and the relative standard deviation was lower than 5.0%. Therefore, MF-MIPs sensor may be a promising method for 4-NP detection in food samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Surface imprinting
Fe3O4 nanoparticles
Fluorescence sensor
4-Nitrophenol
Dual-recognition
Food sample

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and possible sensing principle of the MF-MIPs sensor
Analytical performance of the MF-MIPs sensor
Stability and reusability of the MF-MIPs sensor
Analytical applications of MF-MIPs sensor in real sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the MF-MIPs synthesis.
SEM (A-C) and TEM images (D-I) of Fe3O4 nanoparticles, Fe3O4@SiO2, and MF-MIPs.

FT-IR spectra of MF-MIPs and NIPs (A), XPS spectra (B), magnetic hysteresis loops(C), and TGA curves(D) of Fe3O4, Fe3O4@SiO2 and MF-MIPs. The inset of (C) shows the magnetic responses of MIPs under an external magnetic field.

Fluorescence spectra with 4-NP concentrations from 0 to 60 μmol·L−1 for MF-MIPs (A) and MF-NIPs (B) sensor, and the corresponding quenching amount (F0/F-1) (C) and linear fitting of quenching amount (F0/F-1), with 4-NP concentration from 0 to 10 μmol·L−1 (D).

(A) Fluorescence quenching amounts (F0/F-1) of MF-MIPs and MF-NIPs for 4-NP and its analogs, respectively. (B) Fluorescence quenching amounts (F0/F-1) of MF-MIPs for 4-NP with different common interfering ions. The concentration of all phenols used was 10 μM and the concentration of all interfering ions used was 500 mg L−1. (C) Stability of the MIPs and the fluorescence intensity of MIPs within 6 days. (D) Reusability of MIPs, the fluorescence intensity (a) and the corresponding quenching amount (F0/F-1) (b) of MIPs in each cycle.
Spiked recoveries and RSDs (%, n = 3) for the determination of 4-NP in food samples by the present MF-MIPs sensor.

head.ce:title: A novel magnetic fluorescent molecularly imprinted sensor for highly selective and sensitive detection of 4-nitrophenol in food samples through a dual‐recognition mechanism

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330047, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330047, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Resources, Environmental, and Chemical Engineering, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Resources, Environmental, and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Resources, Environmental, and Chemical Engineering, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, surface imprinting, magnetic separation, and fluorescent detection were integrated to develop a dual-recognition sensor (MF-MIPs), which was used for highly selective and sensitive detection of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) in food samples. Silane-functionalized carbon dots (Si-CDs) participated in the imprinting process and were uniformly distributed into the MIPs layers. MF-MIPs sensor exhibited a high fluorescence response and selectivity based on the dual-recognition mechanism of imprinting recognition and fluorescence identification. The relative fluorescence intensity of MF-MIPs sensor presented a good linear relationship in the range of 0.08–10 μmol·L−1 with a low limit of detection (23.45 nmol·L1) for 4NP. MF-MIPs sensor showed high anti-interference, as well as excellent stability and reusability. The 4-NP recovery from spiked food samples ranged from 93.20 to 102.15%, and the relative standard deviation was lower than 5.0%. Therefore, MF-MIPs sensor may be a promising method for 4-NP detection in food samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, nitrophenols have become a new problem in the food field because of their carcinogenic and genotoxic to humans even at very low concentrations. Nitrophenols are widely used as the synthetic intermediates of pesticides, medicines, dyes, plastics and herbicides (Diamanti-Kandarakis, Bourguignon, Giudice, Hauser, & Prins, 2009; Li et al., 2020). Among these nitrophenols, 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) is a common and important nitrophenol that has been listed as a priority pollutant by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). In drinking water, a maximum concentration of 60 ng/mL has been established for 4-NP (Hao et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2018). Importantly, 4-NP exhibits highly stable properties in the environment and can harm humans via the food chain (Jiang, Liu, Li, Xing, & Ren, 2016). Therefore, a highly sensitive and selective method for the determination of trace 4-NP from food samples is necessary.

To date, many traditional detection methods have been applied to detect trace 4-NP in food samples, such as spectrophotometry (Song et al., 2006), chromatographic techniques (Sukhanov, Kushnir, Churilina, Maslova, & Shatalov, 2017), capillary electrophoresis (Jia, Luo, Song, & Li, 2014) and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). However, these methods showed the disadvantage of extensive sample preparation and separation procedures. Additionally, these methods also suffer from the drawbacks of complicated instruments, expensive or toxic reagents, time-consuming methodology, and low selectivity (Hao et al., 2016; Yu et al., 2017). Thus, it is still a challenge to develop a simple, rapid, and selective method for the determination of trace 4-NP.

Quantum dots (QDs) have been widely used as fluorescent sensors for trace contaminant analysis due to their high luminous intensity, stability, narrow emission spectra, and broad absorption spectra (Chen, Wang, Lu, Wu, & Li, 2016; Yang, Wang et al., 2020). To date, various QDs fluorescence sensors (e.g. CdTe (Han, Li, Wang, & Su, 2014; Jiang et al., 2016; Yu et al., 2017), CdSe (Liu et al., 2018), CdS (Kandi, Behera, Martha, Naik, & Parida, 2019), Mg-ZnS (Luo, Miao, Guo, Sun, & Yan, 2019), MoS2 (Ning et al., 2020) and CDs (Ahmed, Laíño, Calzón, & García, 2015; Hao et al., 2016)) have been developed for highly sensitive 4-NP determination in real samples. Among these fluorescence sensors, CDs based sensors exhibit particular advantages such as low cost, simple synthesis route, better biocompatibility, lower cytotoxicity, high stability, no blinking fluorescence, and tunable excitation and emission spectra (Chen et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2016). However, CDs based sensors often suffer from interference from coexisting substances of 4-NP in real samples, which leads to low selectivity and sensitivity. Thus, combining the merits of high selectivity and excellent sensitivity is a potential method for 4-NP determination.

As a kind of polymers synthesized by functional monomers and templates, molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) have good selectivity, anti-interference, physical stability and chemical stability, as well as low cost and ease of synthesis (Chen et al., 2016; Yu et al., 2017). Traditional MIPs are polymerized by functional monomers, crosslinkers, and polymerization initiators in the presence of templates. Recognition sites complementary in size, shape, and functionality to the template are formed after the removal of the template (L. Chen et al., 2016). To date, many studies have been reported for 4-NP determination by combining MIPs with QDs, and MIP-based QDs fluorescent sensors exhibit both high selectivity of MIPs and high sensitivity of fluorescence detection. However, for most MIP-based QDs fluorescent sensors, MIPs are usually polymerized on the QDs surface and an MIPs layer would form around the QDs (Jiang et al., 2016; Yu et al., 2017). The fluorescence becomes weak if the layer is too thick, thereby reducing the sensing sensitivity, and this is a laborious and time-consuming process. Therefore, some researchers have reported that core–shell structure fluorescent sensors were prepared using the surface imprinted method (Yang, Li et al., 2020; Yang, Li, Wang, Xiong, & Chen, 2019), resulting in strong fluorescence response ability, as well as high binding capacity, high mass transfer, and excellent selectivity. However, it is difficult to separate and recycle these nanoparticle materials from sample solution. Thus, the strategy of surface imprinting on magnetic nanoparticles can be used to overcome the abovementioned limitations for 4-NP determination.

Recently, magnetic nanoparticles have attracted considerable attention to prepare magnetic MIPs because of their advantages of adsorptive selectivity and magnetic sensitivity (Fu, Jin, Bu, & Gui, 2019; Han et al., 2014; Lv, Xie, & Zhang, 2018). Magnetic MIPs can be easily collected and rapidly separated using an external magnetic field. If surface imprinting technology, magnetic response, and CDs fluorescence were integrated to develop a novel sensor, it would exhibit advantages of easy separation, reusability, selectivity, and sensitive detection. However, to the best of our knowledge, the uniform distribution of CDs in the MIPs layer on the magnetic nanoparticle surface to prepare fluorescent sensors for 4-NP determination has rarely been reported.

The nitro group exhibits good fluorescent identification and is typically used as a fluorescent quencher for QDs. Thus, if the MIPs layer of the fluorescent sensor has two imprinted sites (one as a recognition site for hydroxyl groups and the other as fluorescent identification for nitro groups), the specificity and sensitivity can be largely strengthened. However, to date, few works have been based on simultaneous recognition of hydroxyl groups as well as the nitro groups to prepare MIPs based fluorescent sensors for 4-NP determination.

Herein, the combination of MIPs with CDs was polymerized on a magnetic nanoparticle (Fe3O4) surface, which was used as a novel fluorescent sensor (MF-MIPs sensor) for the highly specific and sensitive determination of 4-NP. As shown in Fig. 1 , silane-functionalized carbon dots (Si-CDs) were synthesized using trisodium citrate and APTES as a carbon source and functionalization reagent, respectively (Amjadi & Jalili, 2017; Jalili & Amjadi, 2015). Additionally, the alkoxysilane groups of APTES on Si-CDs surface can be used as crosslinkers for copolymerization with 4-NP and TEOS. Thus, the Si-CDs could uniformly distribute in the MIPs layer, and the imprinted sites were close to the Si-CDs after the removal of 4-NP. Importantly, the MF-MIPs sensor exhibited dual-recognition capability which included imprinted recognition based on hydrogen bonds and fluorescence identification based on nitro quenching agents. The uniform distribution of Si-CDs and dual-recognition capability of imprinted sites could greatly improve the fluorescence response ability and sensitivity of MF-MIPs for 4-NP. In addition, the characterization of MF-MIPs sensor, including the morphology, anti-interference, selectivity, stability, and repeatability was investigated. The practical application of MF-MIPs sensor was used to detect trace 4-NP from food samples.

Hexahydrate ferric chloride (FeCl3·6H2O), trisodium citrate, sodium acetate, ethylene glycol and NH3·H2O were purchased from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shantou, China). 3-aAminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), 2-nitrophenol, 3-nitrophenol, 4-chlorophenol, 3-methyl-4-nitrophenol, hydroquinone, pyrocatechol, resorcinol, 2-nitrotoluene, p-cresol, o-cresol, m-cresol, 4-ethylphenol, butylated hydroxytoluene, and 4-methoxyphenol were all obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals and reagents were of analytical grade and could be used as received without further purification. Ultrapure water with 18.2 MΩ specific resistance (Millipore, USA) was used in all experiments.

Fluorescence emission and UV–vis absorption spectra measurements were performed using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, HITACHI) and UV–vis spectrophotometer (TU-1901, PERSEE, China), respectively. The fluorescence lifetimes were measured on a PL&TRPL fluorescence spectrometer (FSL980, Edinburgh Instruments). Morphological evaluation was performed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-6701F, Japan) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100, Japan). FT-IR analyses were carried out using an FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Nicolet Corp). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250xi, Thermo Scientific) was used to record the XPS spectra. X-ray diffraction (XRD, Smartlab9, Rigaku, Japan) was used to examine the crystalline phase in the 2θ range of 10-80°. Magnetic properties were examined by vibrating specimen magnetometer (VSM, Quantum Design, USA). Thermal gravimetric analyses (TGA) were performed using a differential scanning calorimeter (SDT-Q-600, TA instruments, USA).

The Si-CDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method (Amjadi & Jalili, 2017). Briefly, 8 mmol of trisodium citrate (carbon source) was dissolved in 32 mL of ultrapure water under ultrasound, and 34 mmol of APTES (functionalization reagent) was then injected into the solution and stirred with N2 for 15 min. The resulting precursor was sealed in a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave followed by a hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 2 h. After the reaction, twice the volume of ethanol was added into the mixture and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min and then dissolved again with ultrapure water to purify the solution. The obtained Si-CDs solution was collected and stored at 4 °C for further use.

The Fe3O4@SiO2 microspheres were prepared according to our previous method (Zhu et al., 2018). Briefly, 6.5 mmol of FeCl3, 2 mmol of trisodium citrate, and 26.5 mmol of sodium acetate were dissolved in 60 mL of ethylene glycol with stirring for 30 min. The mixture was sealed in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 10 h. After that, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature and the black products (Fe3O4 nanoparticles) were collected by a magnet. The Fe3O4 nanoparticles were washed with ethanol and ultrapure water for 3 times and dried at 60 °C. 10 mg of Fe3O4 nanoparticles were dispersed into a mixed solution containing 32 mL of ethanol, 8 mL of ultrapure water, and 450 μL of ammonia aqueous solution (25 wt%) and ultrasonicated for 20 min, and then 200 μL of TEOS was added into the mixture and stirred for 6 h at 30 °C. The Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles were washed with ethanol and ultrapure water to remove the blank silica nanoparticles.

10 mg of Fe3O4@SiO2, 150 μL of Si-CDs, and 10 mL of ultrapure water were added into a flask under stirring for 30 min. After that, 4-NP (43 μmol) and APTES (0.13 mmol) were fully mixed with Si-CDs solution and stirred for 60 min, and then TEOS (0.22 mmol) and NH3·H2O (50 μL, 25% in water) were added and mixed for another 12 h. Finally, the crude products were washed 5 times with ethanol and acetonitrile (4/1, v/v) to completely remove the templates. The purified products were collected by an external magnet and dried in an oven to obtain the MF-MIPs sensor. All prepared procedures were carried out in the dark. As a control, non-imprinted polymers (MF-NIPs) were prepared under the same conditions without addition of 4-NP.

The quantum chemistry calculations were performed using a Gaussian 09 software package (Gaussian, Inc., Wallingford, CT) (Haginaka et al., 2019). Geometric optimizations were carried out using DFT with the B3LYP method based on DFT at the 6-311G (d, p) level of the basis set (Rohilla, Chaudhary, Kaur, & Shanavas, 2020). The interaction energy (ΔE) was calculated by considering the counterpoise corrections with basis set superposition error (BSSE). For each system, different configurations were screened to locate the most stable structure. The calculated energies were finally corrected by zero-point energies (ZPEs).

The fluorescence spectra were measured with excitation and emission slit widths of 10 nm and an excitation wavelength of 365 nm with a recording emission range of 400–600 nm. The detection conditions (pH value, response time, and MF-MIPs amount) were optimized to achieve the best detection efficiency. Anti-interference experiments were carried out with interference ions at concentration of 500 mg L−1, and the selectivity of the MF-MIPs sensor was also evaluated at the same concentration of 10 μmol·L−1 for 4-NP and its isomers and analogs.

Real samples were utilized to prove the practical applicability of the MF-MIPs sensor for 4-NP detection. Drinking water and fish (flesh and heads) from the Ganjiang River of Jiangxi province were used as the real samples. The fish samples (2 g) were spiked with different 4-NP concentrations (0, 50, 100, and 200 nmol) and mixed overnight. The mixtures were thoroughly ground up, dispersed into buffer solution (pH 8, 20 mL in total, divided into four times), centrifuged, and then filtered to remove the suspended particles. The water samples were also treated according to the fish samples procedure without grinding. Finally, the 4-NP recoveries of spiked samples (1 mL) were all determined using the MF-MIPs sensor according to the optimized detection conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and possible sensing principle of the MF-MIPs sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation process of the MF-MIPs sensor via the sol–gel method is schematically illustrated in Fig. 1. First, silane-functionalized carbon dots (Si-CDs) with high fluorescence properties were synthesized using trisodium citrate and APTES as the carbon source and functionalization reagent, respectively. Then, the Fe3O4 nanoparticles were prepared using a solvothermal reaction followed by the addition of TEOS to protect the magnet. Finally, the functional monomer (APTES), template molecule (4-NP), crosslinker (TEOS and Si-CDs), and catalyst (NH3·H2O) were polymerized on the Fe3O4 nanoparticles surface via sol–gel polymerization. Because of the alkoxysilane groups, Si-CDs can be used as crosslinkers and then uniformly distributed in the MIPs layer. The developed MF-MIPs sensor exhibited dual-recognition capability, including imprinted recognition based on hydrogen bonds and fluorescence identification based on nitro quenching agents. The uniform distribution of Si-CDs and dual-recognition capability improve the fluorescence response ability and sensitivity of the MF-MIPs sensor for 4-NP determination.

To evaluate the interaction between 4-NP and the imprinted site, the Gaussian 09 program was used to calculate the molecular interaction energies between 4-NP and APTES. The molecular structure of 4-NP and APTES was optimized at the DFT/6-311G (d, p) level. The amino group on APTES may combine with 4-NP in the following two ways: (a) binding to the nitro group and (b) binding to the phenolic hydroxyl group (Fig. S1). The calculated d (–NO2--N-NH2) (2.3392 and 3.2131 Å) was greater than that of d(H-OH--N-NH2) (1.7800 Å), while the interaction energies were −7.59 kJ·mol−1 and −48.83 kJ·mol−1 for way (a) and (b), respectively. Therefore, the interaction can be formed via a hydrogen bond between the phenolic hydroxyl group of 4-NP and the amino group of APTES (by way of b).

To gain insights into the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the fluorescence quenching profile of the MF-MIPs sensor was calculated with the following Stern-Volmer equation (Hao et al., 2016).
F 0
/ F = 1 + K s v
[ c ]
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the MF-MIPs sensor in the absence and presence of 4-NP, respectively. Ksv is the quenching constant with 4-NP, and [c] is the concentration of 4-NP.

The fluorescence lifetime of the MF-MIPs sensor was measured before and after the recognition of 4-NP. The fluorescence lifetime of MF-MIPs was 5.85 ns after the recognition of 4-NP, which was shorter than that of MF-MIPs without recognition (7.65 ns) (Fig. S2). The altered fluorescence lifetime further confirmed dynamic quenching (Guo, Zhang, Cao, & Leng, 2016). At the same time, the Stern-Volmer plots at different temperatures are shown in Fig. S3A and the Ksv values at the three temperatures are presented in Table S1. It was found that the Ksv value increased when the temperature increased from 5 to 65℃. A rising temperature could accelerate the collision speed of compounds and then speed up the dynamic binding process (Wu et al., 2020), which gives a preliminary judgment that the probable complex interaction of MF-MIPs with 4-NP is dynamic collision rather than static quenching. The UV–vis absorption spectra of the 4-NP, Si-CDs, and 4-NP/Si-CDs complexes are shown in Fig. S3B, it can be seen that no ground state complex was generated between 4-NP and Si-CDs, which confirms that fluorescence quenching of the MF-MIPs sensor by 4-NP was the main a dynamic quenching process.

Fig. S4A shows the emission spectrum of the MF-MIPs sensor (a) and the absorption spectrum of 4-NP (b). Only a small part of overlap was found, which led to little efficiency of energy transfer. Therefore, it is proposed that electron transfer would occur between 4-NP and Si-CDs during the fluorescence quenching process. Under UV excitation at 365 nm, Si-CDs become excited and have a strong redox ability (Fig. S4B). An excited state complex (exciplex) formed when 4-NP rebounded into the imprinted site and approached Si-CDs. Then, the energy of Si-CDs would be transferred to the exciplex, resulting in fluorescence quenching. Furthermore, the molecular orbital theory was used to explain the quenching mechanism (Fig. S4C). As seen, the electrons of Si-CDs could accept UV energy and then would be excited from the ground state (valence band) to the conduction band and transit to the initial condition to generate the blue emission (Fig. S4C (a)). The UV–vis absorption of 4-NP was at approximately 229 and 400 nm at pH 8 (Fig. S4A(b)), which was very closed to the conduction band gap of the Si-CDs (Fig. S4A(c)) and the interaction force led to electron transfer between the Si-CDs and 4-NP. Thus, with the existence of 4-NP, the electrons at the conductive band of the Si-CDs could directly jump into the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of UV and the visible band of the exciplex. Since all the energy bands of the exciplex were higher than those of the Si-CDs, the excited electrons of the Si-CDs could return back to the ground state without emitting fluorescence, which would lead to fluorescence quenching (Fig. S4C (b)).

The morphologies of the Fe3O4 nanoparticles, Fe3O4@SiO2, and MF-MIPs sensor were characterized by SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 A, D and G, Fe3O4 nanoparticles exhibited a well-dispersed spherical shape with a rough surface, which was attributed to the fact that Fe3O4 nanoparticles were formed by a cluster of many nanocrystals. Compared with Fe3O4 nanoparticles, Fe3O4@SiO2 (Fig. 2B, E and H) exhibited some aggregation with a smooth surface and the diameter was approximately 170 nm, indicating that Fe3O4 was coated with a silica layer with approximately 10 nm. After polymerization, the MF-MIPs sensor showed an imprinted layer on the surface of Fe3O4@SiO2 with an approximately 185 nm diameter, showing that the MIPs layer was approximately 15 nm (Fig. 2C, F and I).

FT-IR spectra of MF-MIPs and MF-NIPs sensors are shown in Fig. 3 A. MF-MIPs after 4-NP removal and MF-NIPs showed similar spectra. The broad peak at approximately 1095 cm−1 and the characteristic peaks at 800 and 465 cm−1 were attributed to the Si-O-Si asymmetric stretching and the Si-O vibrations, respectively. The wide absorption band at 3421 cm−1 could be ascribed to the OH stretching vibration of SiO2. A characteristic absorption of CN bonding appeared at 1470 cm−1, and the bands at 3386, 1630 and 1560 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration of the NH of the amino group. These results also confirmed that the MF-MIPs sensor was successfully synthesized. After rebinding 4-NP, the appearance of a peak at 853.5 cm−1 could be attributed to the para-substitution of the benzene ring from 4-NP, demonstrating the successful embeddedness of 4-NP in the recognition site.

The surface groups of Fe3O4 nanoparticles, Fe3O4@SiO2 and MF-MIPs were investigated using XPS. Elemental analysis indicated that the atom amounts of C1s, Si2p, O1s and N1s of the imprinting layer were 34.73, 19.95, 41.02 and 4.30, respectively (Table S2). The full-scan XPS spectra showed five peaks at 103.07, 284.8, 399.51, 532.31 and 711.56 eV (Fig. 3B), which were attributed to Si2p, C1s, N1s, O1s and Fe2p, respectively. Fe3O4 nanoparticles showed obvious Fe2p and O1s peaks, and the C1s peak came from contamination or other carbon sources as a calibration. The high-resolution XPS spectrum in the Fe2p (Fig. S5A) showed that the two peaks located at approximately 712.3 and 710.6 eV correspond to Fe2p3/2, while the peaks at 725.2 and 723.5 eV correspond to Fe2p1/2. The high XPS spectra of N1s (Fig. S5B) revealed the existence of C-NH2, CNC, and CNC bonds, which showed sharp peaks at 400.2, 401.3, and 402.6 eV, respectively, indicating that nitrogen exists in MF-MIPs (Fu et al., 2019).

XRD was used to determine the crystalline structure of Fe3O4 nanoparticles, Fe3O4@SiO2 and MF-MIPs in the 2θ range of 10-80°. As shown in Fig. S6, six diffraction peaks of Fe3O4 (2θ = 30.31°, 35.56°, 43.14°, 53.46°, 57.12°, and 62.67°) were observed in all samples. The peak positions at the corresponding 2θ values were indexed as (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (440), which matched well with the database of magnetite in the JSPDS-International Center for Diffraction Data for Fe3O4 crystal (JCPDS #19-0629). The intensity of these peaks decreased for Fe3O4@SiO2 and MF-MIPs, which was due to silica coating and MIP layer grafting on the magnetite cores.

The magnetic properties of Fe3O4 nanoparticles, Fe3O4@SiO2, and MF-MIPs were characterized by measuring magnetization curves using VSM. In Fig. 3C, the magnetic hysteresis loop curves of the three samples present nearly no hysteresis and zero coercivity, which indicated that the MF-MIPs sensor had the ability for quick aggregation as well as homogeneous suspension during fluorescence measurement process. The saturation magnetization (Ms) values of Fe3O4 nanoparticles, Fe3O4@SiO2, and MF-MIPs were 70.5, 56.5, and 49.1 emu·g−1, respectively. The decrease in magnetic saturation of MF-MIPs might be attributed to the SiO2 and imprinted shells on the Fe3O4 surface. Under an external magnetic field, MF-MIPs can be rapidly separated to the wall and the dispersion can become clear and transparent within 30 s (inset of Fig. 3C). These results showed that the MF-MIPs sensor has superparamagnetic properties, which improved the separation efficiency and simplified the experimental process.

The TGA results of Fe3O4 nanoparticles, Fe3O4@SiO2, and MF-MIPs are shown in Fig. 3D. The results showed that the TGA curve of Fe3O4 was almost a straight line with only a 9.08 percent reduction, suggesting that Fe3O4 nanoparticles were almost pure without any solvents. There was a slight weight loss of 24.07% at 200–700 °C for Fe3O4@SiO2 microspheres, which may be due to the dehydration of the SiO2 layer. MF-MIPs exhibited a larger weight loss of 31.64%, indicating that the whole imprinted shell completely decomposed under high temperature. These characterization results confirmed that the MF-MIPs sensor was successfully prepared in this study.

The pH effect on the fluorescence quenching ability of the MF-MIPs sensor in the presence of 4-NP solution (10 μmol·L−1) was investigated in the range of 5–9 (Fig. S7A). The results showed that the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F-1) rapidly increased from 0.14 to 0.62 as the pH value increased from 5.0 to 8.0 and then changed slightly from 0.62 to 0.59 in the range of 8.0–9.0. The NH2 group of APTES tended to increasingly deprotonate from pH 5.0 to 8.0, thus enhancing the electrostatic repulsion among MF-MIPs, and leading to fluorescence quenching efficiency. The slight change may be due to the deprotonation equilibrium of NH3 and COOH groups. The pH values of environmental water samples were usually neutral or slightly alkaline. Therefore, pH 8.0 was the optimal pH for the following experiments.

The MF-MIPs amount in the sensing system was investigated and the results are shown in Fig. S7B. The fluorescence intensity increased from 407 to 1078 with increasing MF-MIPs concentration from 20 to 80 μg·mL−1, and then the increase slowed down from 1078 to 1094 due to the inner filter effect within a concentration of 80–100 μg·mL−1 (S. Chen, Yu, & Wang, 2018) (Fig. S7B(a)). The fluorescence quenching efficiency was obtained after adding 4-NP (10 μmol·L−1), and the quenching efficiency (F0/F-1) was highest at 50 μg·mL−1 MIF-MIPs (Fig. S7B(b)). Thus, 50 μg·mL−1 was chosen as the concentration for the MF-MIPs sensor system.

The response time of the MF-MIPs sensor system was also optimized to further evaluate the accessibility to the recognition sites. The fluorescence quenching efficiency change of MF-MIPs in the presence of 4-NP solution (10 μmol·L−1) was recorded at different times (Fig. S7C). The quenching rate (F0/F-1) increased rapidly from 0 to 0.54 in the initial 1 min and reached equilibrium (0.61) within 2–5 min. The fast response time may be due to the uniform distribution of Si-CDs into the imprinted shell and dual-recognition capability of MF-MIPs. The fluorescence quenching efficiency was relatively unchanged after 2 min. Therefore, 2 min was used as the optimized response time for the detection of 4-NP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the MF-MIPs sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal conditions, the capability of MF-MIPs for quantitative determination of 4-NP was further investigated. The fluorescence spectra of the MF-MIPs sensor were recorded after incubation in different concentrations of 4-NP for 2 min. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity of MF-MIPs was obviously reduced from 0 to 60 μmol·L-1 for 4-NP with a red-shifting peak. Increasing amounts of 4-NP enter the imprinted sites and around the 4-NP with increasing concentration; therefore, the imprinted layers around the Si-CDs change denser, resulting in the peak red-shifting. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity of MF-NIPs (Fig. 4B) was slightly reduced under the same conditions as MF-MIPs. These results indicated that the specific recognition sites for 4-NP were formed on the surface of MF-MIPs. The MF-MIPs presented excellent linearity over the range of 0.08–10 μmol·L−1 with a correlation coefficient of 0.997 for 4-NP (Fig. 4C and D). Consequently, a high imprinting factor (IF = KMF-MIPs/KMF-NIPs) of 12.2 could be obtained. The limit of detection (LOD) was obtained with a value of 23.45 nmol·L−1 based on LOD = 3σ/s, which was lower than the LHA level (0.43 μmol·L−1) (Yu et al., 2017). Therefore, the developed MF-MIPs sensor showed great potential for trace 4-NP detection in real samples with high sensitivity.

The selectivity of the MF-MIPs sensor was evaluated using isomers and analogs of phenolic and nitrobenzene compounds (Fig. S8). Although their chemical structure was similar to that of 4-NP, the fluorescence quenching efficiencies of these analogs were all lower than that of 4-NP (Fig. 5 A). It can be seen that MF-MIPs had a specific recognition capability for 4-NP, and the specificity was probably due to the existence of imprinted cavities in MF-MIPs. Obviously, the quenching efficiency of 3-methyl-4-nitrophenol was higher than that of the other analogs. However, MF-MIPs seem to have no recognition ability for nitrophenol (2-nitrophenol and 3-nitrophenol) or p-substituted phenols (4-chlorophenol, hydroquinone, p-cresol, and 4-ethylphenol), which further confirmed our previous hypothesis as follows: only para-phenols could enter the imprinted site. The MF-MIPs sensor only responds to the nitro group, and there is no response even for other para-phenolic analogs. This result indicated that the recognition mechanism of MF-MIPs is based on the dual recognition of the nitro-phenolic group and the para-position conformation. The interference of potentially different common interfering ions was also investigated, showing that all of the potentially interfering ions have no effect on the fluorescence quenching (Fig. 5B). These results indicated that the MF-MIPs sensor exhibited excellent selectivity towards 4-NP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and reusability of the MF-MIPs sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of the MF-MIPs sensor was investigated by repeatedly measuring the fluorescence intensity within 6 days (Fig. 5C). The fluorescence intensity was almost unchanged within 6 days, suggesting that the MF-MIPs sensor exhibited excellent physical and chemical stability due to the protection of imprinted shells. Reusability plays a vital role in MF-MIPs sensor to extend practical applications. The removal-rebinding cycle was repeated five times using the same MF-MIPs sensor. Fig. 5D shows that the MF-MIPs sensor can be regenerated and reused because of low RSDs (0.60–3.08%). Therefore, the MF-MIPs sensor can be reused with practical and cost-effective applications in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical applications of MF-MIPs sensor in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: The MF-MIPs were used for the determination of 4-NP in real food samples. In this study, the 4-NP concentration of real samples was determined using the MF-MIPs sensor and HPLC-UV. As seen from Table 1, 4-NP was not detected in the water and fish samples using HPLC. However, the 4-NP amount was 0.0127 μmol·L−1, 0.0182 μmol·L−1 and 0.0237 μmol·L−1 for water, flesh of fish and head of fish using the MF-MIPs sensor, respectively. These results indicated that the MF-MIPs sensor is an accurate and reliable method for 4-NP determination with higher sensitivity than the HPLC method. The real samples were spiked with different 4-NP concentrations and the results are listed in Table 1 . The satisfactory recoveries ranged from 93.20% to 102.15% with RSDs lower than 5.0% for these spiked samples. Therefore, such MF-MIPs sensor exhibit great potential for 4-NP detection in food samples.

The performance of the MF-MIPs sensor for the detection of 4-NP was compared with previous QDs based sensors (Table S3) (Ahmed et al., 2015; Hao et al., 2016; Jiang et al., 2016; Li et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2015; Wei et al., 2015; Yu et al., 2017). The MF-MIPs showed excellent sensitivity for 4-NP detection with the lowest LOD compared to previous QDs based sensors. In addition, the MF-MIPs exhibited a rapid fluorescence response within 2 min because of the uniform distribution of CDs in MIPs layers. Additionally, the IF value (12.2) of MF-MIPs was the highest compared with previous reports, which was due to the dual-recognition mechanism. Moreover, the magnetic response of MF-MIPs could simplify the detection processes and easily be recycled. Therefore, our MF-MIPs sensor offers remarkable advantages such as rapidity, high sensitivity and selectivity, and good reliability and practicability.

In summary, a novel magnetic and fluorescent molecularly imprinted polymers (MF-MIPs) sensor was constructed for highly selective and sensitive fluorescence detection of 4-NP through a dual‐recognition mechanism. The MF-MIPs sensor integrated the merits of the magnetic responses of Fe3O4 nanoparticles, the high sensitivity of the fluorescence property, and the selectivity of MIPs. The MF-MIPs sensor has a good linear range of 0.08–10 μmol·L−1 and a low limit of detection (3σ/k) of 23.45 nmol·L−1. Additionally, the MF-MIPs sensor exhibited excellent selective and sensitive responses to 4-NP. In real food samples, the MF-MIPs sensor had excellent capacity for 4-NP detection with satisfactory detection recoveries and low RSDs. Therefore, the presented MF-MIPs sensor may provide a promising method for the determination of trace 4-NP in food samples with simple, rapid, reliable and recycled use.

Wenting Zhu: Methodology, Writing-Review & editing. Yushun Zhou: Methodology. Shuai Liu: Software. Mei Luo: Investigation. Jun Du: Data curation. Jieping Fan: Formal analysis. Hua Xiong: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources. Hailong Peng: Supervision, Project administration, Writing-Review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31860470; 31660482 and 21667018).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2021.129126.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
